content.full_text:
DONALD G. VANLUE
22782 THIRD ST.
HAYWARD, CA 94541
(415) 581-6178

February 23, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

Enclosed please find a copy of a letter sent to the
Metropolitan Transportation Commission by Sherman Lewis
who is employed by California State University, Hayward;
and who is a spokesman and chairman of a group who call
themselves the "Hayward Area Planning Committee". This
group is responsible for recently attempting to place a
referendum on the ballot in the City of Hayward, and have
since filed a lawsuit to defend , or correct, an error in
the wording of the proposed referendum. This group has
been active for many years , both supporting and opposing,
publicly, various city measures and candidates.

To the best of my knowledge, or that of informed people
within the City, they have failed to provide any information as to funds raised or spent in their political
activities. Also, as seen by the enclosed letter, they
are using University stationery to publicize their position
and influence voters.

My question to you is, ... are these activities by this
group exempt from identifying themselves publicly, and
registering and reporting according to F.P.P.C. standards,
and if so, why?

Very truly yours,

Donald G. Vanlue

RECEIVED
FFB 15 1990
CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY HAYWARD
HAYWARD, CALIFORNIA 94542-9988

SCHOOL OF ARTS, LETTERS, AND SOCIAL SCIENCES
Department of Political Science
Telephone: (415) 881-3221

Attn.: Public Information                          February 13, 1990
MTC
101 8th St.
Oakland CA 94607                    RE: TCMs for Contingency Plan

Dear sirs:

     Res. 2107: It is disappointing that MTC is not willing to
delay a single freeway despite the clear requirements of its
Contingency Plan and ample evidence that freeways have induced
dispersed, car-dependent growth. The only comfort might be that
the procedures for considering projects for possible delay will
be so onerous that they cause the delay MTC is claiming to avoid.

     Res. 2107 Attachment A parts E and F rely too much on environ-
mental documentation paid for by project sponsors. Such reports
generally characterize project-caused pollution as insignificant
and underestimate long term traffic growth. It would be interest-
ing to see how many old ERs claimed no pollution problems would
occur on facilities now causing much pollution. MTC generally
passes the buck to local governments' land use decisions, but has
not used its powers to influence those decisions. Nor has MTC
studied pricing issues at all seriously; what little research it
has done has not led to any policy proposals with the exception
of bridge tolls.

     The criterion in Attachment A part E c) is clearly inconsis-
tent with clean air goals. MTC should compare the project to a
comparably priced alternative using pricing reforms, transit
improvements, and land use reforms for balance, density, mixed
use, and non-LOV modes. Too often a project with smooth flow
based on under-projected traffic is compared to a no-build alter-
native with congested flow based on over-projected traffic.

     MTC promises in Attachment A part E to evaluate all projects,
but does not indicate any interest in the growth inducing impacts
of freeways. Part F considers only projects whose environmental
documents show significant negative impact. It is not clear
whether "environmental documentation" is that produced by the
project sponsors or also includes additional analysis prepared by
MTC. It is not clear at the top of p. 6 from "will be evaluated"
who will do the evaluation. Generally, the passive voice obscures
MTC's responsibility, if any is intended.

     Even if a project has significant negative impact, MTC will
still not delay the project or even study a comparably priced
alternative, but only help the sponsor find some TCMs to "miti-
gate" impacts.


                    THE CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY

Attachment B further clarifies that MTC has no intention of
delaying any projects regardless of environmental impact.
The procedures assume that impacts can be adequately "mitigated"
there simply is no procedural category of "delayed projects." The
best MTC could come up with is "proposed projects to be delayed
until..." Part F on p. 4 is thus a mystery as it refers to a
"recommended delay list" that procedures do not produce.
Perhaps the "delay and design review" is a euphemism for the delay
list designed to show sensitivity to MTC's major clients.

The Attachment B part B. discussion seems to be technically
deficient. CO is not a localized air pollutant. It is everywhere
Background levels are major part, of any local
level. Locally exceeds are regionally caused. Regarding ozone,
I called EPA about the "suggested test" and they seem to think
that MTC should actually do something for clean air.

The design review list on p. 2 of Attachment B is too short,
given the deficiency of agencies to ignore what is not listed.
"Adjacency to" is too narrow; all new development using the facil-
ity should be subject to mitigation requirements. If the full
range of TCMs listed in other MTC documents were adequately
implemented, the need for the highway facility would evaporate.
In that case the TCMs could not be part of project design because
there would be no project, but rather an alternative project
meeting the same need in a manner consistent with air quality.

Appendix 1 to Attachment B is too simplistic in "3. Defining
Project Alternatives." A true alternative is one that meets the
Project Alternatives without increasing LOV vmt and trips. No where does MTC
need
admit that such is possible.

Appendix 1 part 5 on methodology seems to ignore that MTC trip
tables are the major assumption used in all forecasts. Part 5 at
least, for the first time, mentions transit, saying it "should be
considered." This clarion call, however, is not related to land
use and pricing reforms, and is thus incomplete.

Generally, Res. 2107 is so unsophisticated in its approach to
transportation problems and so uncomprehending of alternatives
that it fails to meet the requirements of the Contingency Plan
and Clean Air Act.

TCMs: The distributed plan had no tonnages; I was able to get
them from Greig Harvey at the JAQPC meeting Feb. 8. The analysis
seems very sophisticated but does not add up to enough tonnage
reduction. The biggest potential seems to be to beef up the I/M
program, which is not listed. My other comments are in the CATS
letter.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Sherman Lewis

[The image appears to be almost entirely blank or extremely faded, with no clearly legible text visible. Any text that may be present is too faint or unclear to transcribe accurately.]
